NYC hero — street-level Manhattan, evening light
New York City · Summer 2026
Build something real in the city that never stops building

Two weeks. Three tracks. One cohort. Work alongside practitioners in product, business, and go-to-market — in a city where every block teaches you something about how things get made.

June 27Start Date
July 11End Date
24Students
3Tracks

This Isn't a Summer Class

Hudson Lab is a working studio. You won't sit in lectures. You'll build real projects — prototypes, business cases, campaign strategies — with guidance from people who've actually shipped things.

New York is the context, not the backdrop. You'll use the city as a research lab: interviewing strangers, testing ideas in real markets, learning from a place that doesn't slow down for anyone.

Students working in studio space
Full-width: NYC neighborhood street, daytime

Your Two Weeks

Week one builds the foundation. Week two ships the work.

Week 1

Research & Define

Mon–TueTeam formation, partner briefing, foundation sessions. Meet your challenge sponsor and understand the problem space.
Wed–ThuUser research, market analysis, competitive landscape. Get out of the studio and into the city — interviews, observations, data gathering.
FriProblem definition. Synthesize your research. Present your framing to the cohort and get cross-track feedback.
Week 2

Build & Present

Mon–TuePrototyping, modeling, campaign building. Each track produces its deliverable — product prototypes, business cases, go-to-market strategies.
Wed–ThuIteration and testing. Refine based on feedback. Rehearse presentations. Polish your work to a professional standard.
FriFinal presentations to corporate executives. This is real — the people in the room make decisions based on what you show them.

Your Challenge Partner

Columbia Business School — Climate Knowledge Initiative. This summer's NYC challenge focuses on sustainability and the future of energy employment. You'll work with climate experts from Columbia and one of Europe's leading chemical companies to explore how industries can build a workforce capable of reaching net zero.

This isn't a case study from a textbook. It's an active question the Tamer Institute is working to answer — and your team's thinking will be part of that conversation.

Columbia University campus / Tamer Institute

Getting There

Travel Together

International students are met by an HLV chaperone and travel together to the program location. You won't navigate this alone.

Housing on Campus

Stay in Columbia University residence halls on the Morningside Campus. 24/7 security, Resident Advisors, dining access. Day-student option also available.

Your Evenings

NYC is your classroom after hours. Curated neighborhood guides, restaurant recommendations, cultural events. Optional group dinners. Always supervised when organized by HLV.

Three Tracks

Pick the one that matches how you think.

01
Product

Design & Build

Turn problems into prototypes. User research, IA, interaction design. Ship something testable by week two.

02
Business

Model & Validate

Make the numbers work. Revenue models, unit economics, competitive analysis.

03
Go-To-Market

Launch & Grow

Get it in front of people. Positioning, channels, campaign execution.

I came in thinking I wanted to do product. By day four, I realized I was a business person who happened to like design.
NYC 2025

Your NYC Facilitators

Photo
Product Track

Stacey Seltzer

A decade of shipping product at scale. Stacey designed HLV's Product track — exercises built from real product decisions.

Photo
Business Track

Takeshi

Business strategy rooted in real operations. He asks "but do the unit economics work?" until you can answer with confidence.

Photo
Go-To-Market Track

Luke

Growth and go-to-market from the ground up. Bias toward measurement — if you can't track it, you can't improve it.
